w:r.w:t: Flowfiy is a multi-tenant B2B SaaS platform that automates the entire outbound sales pipeline using a five-stage AI agent system powered by Anthropic’s Claude. The platform follows a Managed AI model — Flowfiy manages Claude centrally while users supply their own credentials for Apollo, Apify, Gmail, and Calendly — keeping API costs off-platform and enabling unlimited scale.

The system ingests a business profile, autonomously identifies ideal customer profiles, discovers qualified leads via Apollo.io, researches each company via website scraping, scores and ranks prospects, and generates hyper-personalized outreach emails — all without human intervention.
Zero manual prospecting — AI discovers, qualifies, and writes for you
Managed AI — Claude is fully managed by Flowfiy, no setup required
50 free lead generations to start — no credit card required
Full multi-tenancy — team workspaces with role-based access
Seamless Gmail integration with Calendly booking links
B2B sales teams, founders, growth agencies
Flowfiy is deployed across four managed cloud services, each handling a distinct responsibility. This separation keeps concerns clean and allows each layer to scale independently.
Always-on Node.js process for AI pipelines
Direct URL for migrations, pooled for app
Redis-backed BullMQ job queues + rate limiting
The following text diagram describes the data flow between layers:
32-byte hex key for AES-256-GCM credential encryption
Google OAuth2 client ID for Gmail
App base URL (used in auth redirects)
React framework, server components, API routes
Component library built on Radix UI primitives
Animation library for landing page transitions
Server-side auth helpers for Next.js App Router
Redis-backed job queues (lead gen + email send workers)
Redis client with TLS support for Upstash
Sliding-window rate limiting on API routes
TypeScript execution for Railway worker (no compile step)
All 5 AI agents (ICP, Company, Qualification, Personalization)
People/company search, lead discovery with filters
Send outreach emails on behalf of user
Inject booking links into outreach copy

Every resource in Flowfiy is scoped to an Organization. A user can belong to multiple organizations and switch between them. All database queries include an organizationId filter, and Supabase Row Level Security (RLS) policies provide an additional enforcement layer at the database level.
|-- OrganizationMember (users in this org, with role: OWNER | ADMIN | MEMBER)
Full access. Can manage billing, invite/remove members, delete org, manage all integrations
All data operations (leads, campaigns, integrations). Cannot delete org or manage billing
View + create leads and campaigns. Cannot manage integrations or billing

All tables with an organization_id column are protected by RLS policies that check the authenticated user’s membership in that organization before allowing any SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE operation. This prevents any cross-tenant data leakage even in the event of an application bug.

Users store their external API credentials inside Flowfiy’s Integration model. These credentials are encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM before being written to the database. The encryption key is stored only in the server environment (never in client code) and rotated per deployment.
Algorithm: AES-256-GCM (authenticated encryption — provides confidentiality and integrity)
Key: 32-byte random hex string stored in ENCRYPTION_KEY environment variable
IV: 12-byte random nonce generated per encryption call
Auth tag: 16-byte GCM authentication tag appended to ciphertext
User enters API key in UI

The core of Flowfiy is a sequential five-agent pipeline, each powered by a dedicated Claude system prompt. Agents pass structured data (TypeScript interfaces) between each other. The pipeline runs entirely inside the Railway worker process as part of a BullMQ job.
Input: company name, service, ICP description, target industries/geos, pain points
Output: refined ICP, Apollo search filters (job titles, industries, company sizes),
Input: Apollo search filters from ICP Analyzer
Output: raw lead records (name, email, title, company, LinkedIn URL)
[Agent 2: Company Analyzer] (per lead, batched 5 at a time)
Input: company name, website, industry, size, scraped content, ICP summary
Output: business model summary, tech stack, growth signals, pain point matches,
Input: lead details, company analysis, ICP summary, qualification criteria
Output: qualified (bool), score (0-100), best angle, pain point match,
Input: lead info, business profile, best angle, hooks, Calendly link
Output: subject line, email body, follow-up 1, follow-up 2
Results saved to: LeadResearch + OutreachCopy tables

Leads are processed in batches of 5 with Promise.all within each batch. This provides parallelism while respecting Claude API rate limits. A lead that fails processing (network error, Claude error) is individually caught and marked DISQUALIFIED without failing the entire job.
Refine ICP from business description, map to Apollo filters
Analyze company from website + industry data vs ICP
Score lead against ICP criteria, find personalization angles
JSON: qualified, score, bestAngle, painPointMatch, hooks[]
Flowfiy uses BullMQ backed by Upstash Redis for all long-running asynchronous operations. Two queues are defined: one for the AI lead generation pipeline and one for Gmail email delivery.
Job data: { organizationId, leadListId, leadsPerRun, mode: 'apollo' | 'import' }
Status progression: DRAFT → QUEUED → RESEARCHING → READY (or FAILED)
jobStatus field tracks sub-step: analyzing_icp → discovering_leads → analyzing_companies → complete
On completion: generationCount incremented, qualifiedLeads count updated
Job data: { organizationId, campaignLeadId, leadId, outreachCopyId }
Uses user’s Gmail OAuth tokens (refreshed automatically if expired)
Records gmailMessageId and gmailThreadId on CampaignLead after send
Status set to SENT; reply tracking via webhook or polling
All BullMQ clients connect to Upstash Redis over TLS using ioredis. The connection is created once and reused:
All API routes are Next.js Route Handlers located under src/app/api/. They run as serverless functions on Vercel with a maximum duration of 300 seconds.
Supabase OAuth callback — exchanges code for session, redirects to /onboarding or /dashboard
Create org during onboarding. Validates user has no existing org.
List all integrations with status (credentials redacted)
Handle Gmail OAuth callback, store tokens
Enqueue lead generation job, check usage limits
Fetch leads for a list with pagination + filters
Import leads from CSV (creates LeadList + Lead records, enqueues job in import mode)
AI chat endpoint for lead Q&A (uses Claude with lead context)
Regenerate outreach copy for a specific lead
Handle Razorpay webhook events (subscription.activated, payment.captured, etc.)
2. supabase.auth.signUp({ email, password }) — Supabase creates auth.users record
5. Callback exchanges code for session, sets secure cookies
6. Middleware checks: no org membership → redirect to /onboarding
7. Onboarding wizard: create Organization + OrganizationMember (role: OWNER)
The Next.js middleware runs on every non-static request and enforces authentication:
Zero auth work — skip Supabase call entirely (fast path)
If not logged in: redirect to /login

The public-route fast path was specifically added to reduce page load latency by 200–400 ms on marketing pages, which previously paid the full supabase.auth.getUser() network round-trip on every request.

Gmail integration uses Google OAuth2 with offline access to obtain a refresh token. This allows Flowfiy to send emails on behalf of the user at any time — including during background worker jobs — without requiring the user to be logged in.
3. User approves on Google consent screen
└ Encrypt and store in Integration table (type: GMAIL)
5. Worker decrypts tokens, creates gmail client, calls messages.send()
Worker uses googleapis npm package with the user’s stored tokens
Access token automatically refreshed via refresh token if expired
Email constructed as RFC 2822 MIME message, base64url encoded
Gmail message ID and thread ID saved to CampaignLead for tracking
Daily send limit enforced at campaign level (default: 50 emails/day)
All third-party API keys encrypted with AES-256-GCM before database write
Encryption key stored only in server environment variables (never in client bundles)
IV is unique per encryption call — same key never used with same IV
GCM auth tag prevents ciphertext tampering
Credentials never returned to the client (API response strips encrypted fields)
Supabase Row Level Security enabled on all tenant tables
RLS policies verify organizationId against authenticated user’s org membership
DIRECT_URL only used for migration commands, never in application code
Prisma uses parameterized queries (SQL injection prevention)
All API routes: session verified via supabase.auth.getUser() before any data access
Rate limiting: @upstash/ratelimit sliding window on AI-heavy endpoints
Razorpay webhook signature verification before processing billing events
CORS: Next.js defaults apply (same-origin for API routes)
TypeScript: compile-time type safety across all API request/response shapes
Redis connection uses TLS (Upstash enforces encryption in transit)
Environment variables managed through Vercel / Railway encrypted secret stores
No secrets in source code or Git history
Flowfiy’s PostgreSQL schema is managed by Prisma ORM. The following tables are defined in prisma/schema.prisma. All tables use UUID primary keys and snake_case column names.
id, name, slug (unique), plan, razorpay_customer_id, razorpay_subscription_id, generation_count, generation_limit
Tenant root. Plan: FREE | STARTER | GROWTH | AGENCY
user_id references Supabase auth.users. Unique(org, user)
id, organization_id (unique), company_name, service_offered, icp_description, target_industries[], target_geographies[], pain_points_solved, offer_positioning, outreach_tone, icp_analysis_cache
1:1 with org. icp_analysis_cache stores last Claude ICP output as JSON
id, organization_id, type (enum), encrypted_credentials, status, last_validated_at
id, organization_id, name, status, total_leads, qualified_leads, job_id, job_status, job_error
id, lead_list_id, organization_id, first_name, last_name, email, title, company_name, company_website, company_size, industry, linkedin_url, source, raw_data, status, qualification_score
id, lead_id (unique), organization_id, company_analysis, opportunity_angle, pain_point_match, personalization_notes, research_metadata
1:1 with lead. company_analysis is JSON from Company Analyzer agent
id, lead_id, organization_id, channel, subject_line, body, follow_up_1, follow_up_2, version, is_approved
Supports multiple versions per lead. is_approved flag for human review
id, organization_id, lead_list_id, name, status, channel, gmail_from_address, calendly_link, daily_send_limit, sent_count, reply_count, meeting_count
id, campaign_id, lead_id, outreach_copy_id, status, sent_at, gmail_message_id, gmail_thread_id
Append-only log of generation events for usage tracking
id, name (unique), version, content, variables, is_active
Stored AI prompts (for prompt management / A/B testing)
id, organization_id, user_id, action, resource_type, resource_id, metadata, created_at
Append-only security audit trail. Used for compliance and debugging
generationLimit set per plan (FREE=50, STARTER=500, etc.)
UsageEvent records appended on every successful generation batch
Count incremented in incrementGenerationCount() after job completes
Monthly reset handled by Razorpay subscription renewal webhook
Razorpay handles all subscription lifecycle (create, pause, cancel, renew)
Webhook events update Organization.plan and subscriptionStatus
payment.captured → activate plan, subscription.cancelled → downgrade to FREE
Next.js App Router uses folder-based routing with route groups (parentheses) for layout sharing.
Landing page with hero, features, how it works, pricing, testimonials, FAQ
Company mission, team, and technology stack
Contact cards, mailto form, FAQ, social links
AI lead generation use case page
Email + Google OAuth sign-in form. Logged-in users redirected to /dashboard.
Registration form with T&C acceptance. Links to /terms and /privacy.
Multi-step wizard: org name → business profile → ICP → integrations. Shows 50 free generation offer.
Overview: generation usage, recent lead lists, campaign metrics
Lead list manager: create, view, filter, and export lead lists
Individual lead list with lead table, qualification scores, outreach copy preview
Campaign manager: create, activate, pause, view campaign metrics
Connect and validate Managed AI credentials for all 5 integrations
Analytics dashboard: email metrics, reply rates, meeting booking rates
Current plan, usage stats, upgrade CTA, billing portal link
Connected to GitHub repository (master branch = production)
Build command: prisma generate && next build
Prisma and tsx moved to dependencies[] (not devDependencies) because Vercel builds with NODE_ENV=production
TypeScript build errors suppressed via next.config.ts (ignoreBuildErrors: true) for fast iteration
Environment variables set in Vercel dashboard (not in vercel.json)
Custom domain: flowfiy.com (GoDaddy DNS → Vercel nameservers)
www.flowfiy.com: must be assigned to master branch in Vercel Domains settings
Separate Railway service connected to same GitHub repository
Build command (railway.toml): npm install && npx prisma generate
Start command: npx tsx --tsconfig tsconfig.worker.json src/workers/index.ts
Restart policy: ON_FAILURE with 3 max retries
tsconfig.worker.json: separate TypeScript config with paths aliases for Railway
*.tsbuildinfo added to .gitignore to prevent Nixpacks conflicts
Database migrations via prisma db push (development) or prisma migrate deploy (CI)
Row Level Security policies written in Supabase SQL editor
Google OAuth provider configured in Supabase Authentication settings
Email templates customized for Flowfiy branding
Redis instance created in Upstash console, region closest to Railway deployment
UPSTASH_REDIS_URL and UPSTASH_REDIS_TOKEN added to both Vercel and Railway env vars
ioredis TLS connection (rejectUnauthorized: false for Upstash certificate)
@upstash/ratelimit used for API-level sliding-window rate limits
Railway worker picks up and processes

The most impactful performance improvement was adding a fast-path in the Next.js middleware to skip Supabase auth checks for public routes. Previously every page load (including the marketing homepage) called supabase.auth.getUser(), adding 200–400 ms of network latency.
Lead generation: 3 concurrent jobs prevents overwhelming Claude API rate limits
Company analysis inside each job: batches of 5 with Promise.all for parallelism
Email send: 10 concurrent with hard cap of 50/hour to respect Gmail sending limits
Prisma connection pooling via PgBouncer (DATABASE_URL uses ?pgbouncer=true)
DIRECT_URL bypasses PgBouncer for Prisma migrations (which require direct connections)
UUID primary keys for all tables (avoids sequential ID enumeration attacks)
Cascade deletes configured (e.g., deleting an org cascades to all its data)
Managed AI — Claude is managed centrally by Flowfiy, no per-user API key required
Ideal Customer Profile — the description of the perfect target buyer
Node.js job queue library backed by Redis, used for background AI pipeline jobs
Serverless Redis provider with per-request pricing and REST + ioredis access
Row Level Security — Supabase/PostgreSQL feature that enforces data isolation per tenant
Authenticated encryption algorithm used to protect stored API credentials
B2B lead database and sales intelligence platform (user’s own API key used)
Web scraping platform used to extract company website content (user’s own API key used)
Cloud hosting platform for always-on Node.js services (the background worker)
Serverless deployment platform for Next.js (the frontend and API routes)
Hosted PostgreSQL with auth, RLS, and real-time capabilities
Indian payment gateway used for subscription billing (supports INR + global cards)
